figures.gloss: The great goddess of grain who, in her devastating grief over her daughter's abduction, deliberately withheld the harvest, plunging the world into famine until Zeus was forced to intervene and broker Persephone's return.
figures.facet: Unlike the serene Olympian, the Eleusinian Demeter walked the earth as an old woman in mourning. She is a goddess of active refusal, wielding cosmic starvation to bend the patriarchal heavens to a mother's will.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter; numerous Eleusinian dedicatory reliefs.

figures.gloss: The divine daughter abducted by Hades into the earth, who consumed the pomegranate seed that bound her to a dual existence: a third of the year ruling the dark below, the rest ascending to the light.
figures.facet: At Eleusis, she is no mere victim of seasonal metaphor. She is the terrifying and benevolent Queen of the Dead, holding the absolute keys to personal salvation and a blessed afterlife for the initiates.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter; Pindar, Fragment 137; Sophocles, Fragment 837.

figures.gloss: The Eleusinian prince who received the first wheat-ear from Demeter and was sent forth in a chariot drawn by winged dragons to teach the arts of agriculture to the entire world.
figures.facet: He translates the intimate, localized secret of the Mysteries into an exoteric, universal civilizing mission, effectively linking the esoteric salvation of the soul with the physical survival of humankind.
figures.provenance: Attic vase paintings (e.g., the Triptolemos Painter); Pausanias, Description of Greece 1.38.6.

figures.gloss: The divine personification of the joyful, ecstatic cry shouted by the thousands of initiates as they marched the Sacred Way from Athens to Eleusis, bearing myrtle branches and torches.
figures.facet: He is a deity born entirely from the collective, acoustic frenzy of the worshippers. He serves as a fluid liminal guide, leading the initiates physically through the landscape and spiritually into the mystery.

figures.gloss: In the Homeric Hymn, the old palace servant Iambe who broke the grieving, fasting Demeter's silence with ribald jesting; a later Orphic tradition renamed and reshaped the same comic-obscene figure as Baubo, who bares her body to make the goddess laugh.
figures.facet: She introduces radical bodily humor into a narrative of profound divine trauma — the transgressive, joyful obscenity acts as the shock that restarts the frozen life-force of the cosmos; 'Iambe' (the Hymn's own name) and 'Baubo' (the later Orphic name) are related but not strictly identical figures.
figures.provenance: Clement of Alexandria, Protrepticus 2; Orphic fragments; Homeric Hymn to Demeter.

figures.gloss: The mythical swineherd whose pigs were swallowed by the earth when the chasm opened for Hades' chariot. In his honor, initiates threw live piglets into subterranean chasms known as megara.
figures.facet: He grounds the transcendent promise of the Mysteries in visceral, chthonic blood-magic. He represents the stark agricultural law that the putrefaction of flesh within the earth is required to fertilize the seed.
figures.provenance: Pausanias 1.14.3; Clement of Alexandria, Protrepticus 2; scholia on Lucian.

figures.gloss: The sacred drink of barley meal, water, and pennyroyal mint consumed by initiates to break their ritual fast, mirroring Demeter's own refusal of red wine during her mourning.
figures.facet: It replaces the standard Greek practice of animal blood-sacrifice with a direct, internalized physical communion. Swallowing the brew shifts the locus of the sacred experience entirely into the initiate's own body.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter, lines 208-211 (the drink's recipe and Demeter's own refusal of wine); the ergot/entheogen hypothesis (Wasson, Hofmann & Ruck, The Road to Eleusis, 1978) is contested and rests on archaeobotanical finds elsewhere, notably Mas Castellar de Pontos in Iberia, not on vessels recovered at Eleusis itself.

figures.gloss: The immense, roofed initiation hall at the heart of the sanctuary, capable of holding thousands of tightly packed worshippers in profound darkness before the sudden, blinding flash of the epopteia.
figures.facet: Unlike traditional Greek temples designed merely to house a cult statue, this was an inward-facing architectural machine designed specifically to manipulate light, darkness, and collective psychological pressure.
figures.provenance: Archaeological excavations at Eleusis; Plutarch, Life of Pericles.

figures.gloss: The bridegroom of the deep who seizes Kore with Zeus's tacit consent; at Eleusis he was worshipped not by his grim name but as Plouton, 'the Wealthy One' — host of the dead and giver of the earth's buried riches, with his own sanctuary within the precinct.
figures.facet: The euphemism is the theology: naming him for wealth rather than death reframes the underworld as a granary and a treasury, softening the abduction's terror into an economy of hidden abundance.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter; Pausanias, Description of Greece 1.38.5 (the Ploutonion at Eleusis); Strabo, Geography 9.1.9.

figures.gloss: The torch-bearing goddess who alone hears Persephone's cry from her cave during the abduction, and who — once reunited with Demeter's search — thereafter goes before and behind Persephone as she passes between the upper world and the dead each year.
figures.facet: Her Eleusinian role predates the later 'goddess of witchcraft' reputation: here she is a threshold-witness and escort, the third woman in a Demeter-Persephone-Hecate triad sealing the passage between the two goddesses' realms.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter, lines 24-30 and 438-440.

figures.gloss: Infant son of Keleos and Metaneira of Eleusis, nursed in secret by the disguised, grieving Demeter, who anointed him with ambrosia and laid him in the hearth fire each night to slowly burn away his mortality — until his mother's scream at the sight broke the rite and cost him immortality.
figures.facet: The interrupted ritual is the Hymn's own founding story for the Mysteries: having failed to make one child deathless by stealth, Demeter instead founds a public rite offering everyone else a better afterlife, in full view, by initiation instead of secrecy.

figures.gloss: King of Eleusis who received the disguised, mourning Demeter as a guest in his household and, once she revealed her divinity, was commanded to build her a temple and altar upon the hill above the town — becoming the mortal founder of her cult.
figures.facet: He is the myth's human anchor: the goddess's grief and eventual world-threatening anger both play out inside one ordinary royal household before either becomes cosmic and public.

figures.gloss: Queen of Eleusis, wife of Keleos, whose scream on discovering her infant son Demophon held in the hearth-fire ended the goddess's secret attempt at his immortalization — a mother's fear that, in breaking one rite, occasioned the founding of another.
figures.facet: Her fear is not condemned in the Hymn: it is simply mortal, and the narrative uses it to explain, without blame, why humans are given religion instead of magic — ritual instead of a shortcut.

figures.gloss: Wealth personified as a child, born to Demeter and the mortal Iasion in a thrice-ploughed field (per Hesiod) — the abundance of the grain-store given divine form, shown with a cornucopia on Eleusinian votive reliefs beside the Two Goddesses.
figures.facet: He is the Mysteries' exoteric promise made visible: where the epopteia offers secret salvation to the initiated few, Ploutos advertises the same goddesses' gift as ordinary agricultural abundance, open to anyone who farms well.
figures.provenance: Hesiod, Theogony 969-974; the Eleusinian Great Relief (Eleusis Museum); Aristophanes' Wealth, a later comic elaboration.

figures.gloss: The two hereditary Eleusinian priestly clans who administered the Mysteries for roughly a millennium: the Eumolpidae, descended from the mythical Eumolpus, who alone supplied the hierophant ('shower of the holy things'); and the Kerykes ('Heralds'), who supplied the torch-bearer (dadouchos) and the sacred herald.
figures.facet: Authority here is lineage, not merely office: only these two families could ever produce the Mysteries' senior officiants, making the rite's secrecy a hereditary property as much as a religious one.
figures.provenance: Eleusinian inscriptions naming hierophants and dadouchoi by family; Lysias, Against Andocides; Pausanias 1.38.

figures.gloss: The unnamed, unseen sacred objects of the Mysteries, kept sealed in lidded baskets (kistai) and carried in procession along the Sacred Way between the Eleusinion in Athens and the sanctuary at Eleusis, revealed only by the hierophant at the rite's climax.
figures.facet: Their content was never recorded and remains genuinely unknown to scholarship — the best-kept religious secret to survive from classical antiquity, protected by law as much as by reticence: disclosure carried the death penalty.
figures.provenance: Aristophanes, The Frogs; Herodotus, Histories 8.65; the 415 BCE profanation-of-the-Mysteries scandal recorded in Thucydides and Andocides.

figures.gloss: 'The Beholding' — the highest of the Mysteries' three grades, open only to those already initiated in a previous year's telete, said (per the hostile Christian report of Hippolytus) to climax in the hierophant silently displaying a single reaped ear of grain.
figures.facet: The graded structure — myesis, telete, epopteia — turns initiation into a multi-year credential rather than a single event: the Mysteries' deepest promise was available only to the patient and the twice-returning.
figures.provenance: Hippolytus, Refutation of All Heresies 5.8.39; Plato, Phaedrus 250c (the grades of initiation).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The ultimate mother-daughter dyad (the 'Two Goddesses'), whose traumatic separation and joyful reunion dictates both the agricultural year and esoteric salvation.
Intra-tradition: The ingested internal catalyst and the external architectural container for the final revelation.
GOLD SEAM: The explicit local manifestation of the pan-Hellenic goddess, anchoring her to the specific site of the Eleusinian revelation.
GOLD SEAM: The specific Eleusinian mystery-cult avatar of the Greek Queen of the Underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses who undergo a traumatic descent into the underworld, confronting death directly to establish a new cosmic balance.
GOLD SEAM: Chthonic deities whose burial in the earth serves as the divine guarantee of both the grain's resurrection and human immortality.
GOLD SEAM: Grieving mother-figures who wander the earth in despair, seeking to restore the lost divine seed that will ultimately conquer death.
GOLD SEAM: Benevolent, traveling culture-heroes who explicitly gift the staple crop (wheat/maize) to humanity to ensure their civilization and survival.
GOLD SEAM: Historically syncretized by the Athenians, fusing the Eleusinian cry of joy with the pan-Hellenic god of wine and ecstasy.
GOLD SEAM: Divine figures who descend into the realm of the dead and return, offering a formalized path of personal soteriology and eternal life.
GOLD SEAM: Sacred architectures that function simultaneously as physical storage spaces for agriculture and profound cosmological maps.
GOLD SEAM: The vanishing agricultural deities whose sudden disappearance beneath the earth paralyzes all fertility until they are pacified and returned.
GOLD SEAM: The necessary transgressive force—using obscenity or radical humor to shatter an unbearable divine stasis and restart the cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: Highly ritualized, bodily technologies used to deliberately cross the threshold into ecstatic revelation and the spirit realm.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient Mediterranean mother goddess who frantically searches for the missing god of fertility, forcing the restoration of life.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial sowers and culture-heroes who ensure that humanity has both the physical seed and the spiritual knowledge needed to thrive.
GOLD SEAM: Animal-associated chthonic guides or figures structurally tethered to a catastrophic descent into the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: The essential liminal guide required to navigate the perilous threshold between the profane world and the realm of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a grain-goddess whose presence in the crop itself ties her cult directly to the agricultural cycle.
Abductor and bride — the myth's third actor completing the drama the rest of the file already stages.
The same god under his Eleusinian cult-name; the euphemism 'Plouton' softened the abductor into the sanctuary's wealth-giving host.

Ancient and later sources sometimes conflated Plouton (Hades' Eleusinian name) with Ploutos (Demeter's child of grain-wealth) on name-resemblance alone; Hesiod and cult practice keep them distinct figures — a false-cognate, not a real identity, logged so the confusion isn't silently repeated.
Hecate is the one who hears and tells Demeter of Kore's cry, joining her search with torches lit from Helios's fire.
Thereafter Hecate precedes and follows Persephone in her yearly passage between the two worlds, per the Hymn's own closing lines.
Late-antique Neoplatonists built their cosmic Hekate Soteira on this Hymn's torch-bearing mediatrix, transposing the local cult-figure into a metaphysical world-soul principle.
Father and son within the royal household Demeter takes refuge in.
Mother and son — her scream at the hearth-rite is the Hymn's own aition for why the Mysteries exist as a public rite rather than a private immortalization.
Nurse and nursling: the disguised goddess's attempt to immortalize the prince is the episode from which the sanctuary's founding directly follows.
King and queen of Eleusis, joint hosts of the disguised goddess and joint recipients of her instruction to found her temple.
Demeter commands Keleos to build the temple that becomes, in its later Periclean form, the Telesterion itself.
The hierophant, dadouchos, and herald these clans supplied are the officiants who actually ran the rite inside this hall.
The sealed objects are the literal cargo of the procession Iacchus's ecstatic cry leads from Athens to Eleusis.
The graded climax staged inside the one hall built to manipulate darkness, light, and crowd psychology for exactly this moment.
The ingested internal catalyst (breaking the fast) and the multi-year graded structure it feeds into toward the final revelation.
Independent graded mystery-cult architectures whose culminating rank is reserved for those already initiated in earlier stages.
Ploutos is Demeter's child by Iasion per Hesiod — the goddess's grain-wealth given a body and set beside her on Eleusinian reliefs.

Both document literal, attested use of a psychoactive substance (scored opium poppy vs. the barley-and-herb kykeon) in Aegean-region religious ritual — unusual specificity for the ancient Mediterranean record.
the same goddess venerated as Kore in the Eleusinian Mysteries, the two mystery cults sharing her without a single settled account of which borrowed from which.
Diodorus records Carthage's literal, state-sanctioned adoption of the Sicilian Greek cult of Demeter after 396 BCE — a documented historical transplant, not a myth-parallel.
the same 396 BCE adoption installed Kore/Persephone's cult alongside her mother's in the Carthaginian state religion.

dossier.essence: The most famous of the ancient Greek mystery cults: a secret annual initiation into the worship of Demeter and her daughter Kore (Persephone), held at Eleusis some 20 km northwest of Athens from at least the Archaic period until its suppression under the Christian emperors and the sack of the sanctuary in 396 CE. It was open to almost anyone who spoke Greek and was free of blood-guilt — women and men, free and enslaved — and promised its initiates (mystai) a happier lot among the dead. Its culminating secret, revealed in the great hall (Telesterion), was never written down and remains genuinely unknown.
dossier.cosmos: The cult rests on the myth of the 'Homeric Hymn to Demeter': Hades seizes Kore, Demeter's grief blights the earth into famine, and a brokered compromise returns the maiden for part of each year, mapping the grain cycle onto death and return. Eleusis turned this seasonal story into a claim about the human dead — initiates would enjoy a blessed existence in the underworld while the uninitiated lay in darkness and mud. Death is thus not annihilation but a state that ritual experience can improve.
dossier.practice: The rites ran on a two-tier calendar — the Lesser Mysteries near Athens in spring and the Greater Mysteries in autumn (month of Boedromion) — a multi-day sequence of purification in the sea, a procession along the Sacred Way, a fast broken by the barley drink kykeon, and a climactic night in the Telesterion where the hierophant displayed the secret sacred objects (hiera) amid darkness and sudden fire. The hereditary priestly families of the Eumolpidae and Kerykes administered it, and disclosure of the secret was a capital offense.
dossier.canon: There is no scripture; the founding narrative is the anonymous 'Homeric Hymn to Demeter' (c. 7th century BCE), supplemented by scattered testimonia in Pausanias, Clement of Alexandria, and inscriptions — the initiatory secret itself was oral and is lost.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds initiation as the purchase of a better death — religion offering not moral reform but a change in one's posthumous fate through a single overwhelming ritual experience.
sources: George E. Mylonas, 'Eleusis and the Eleusinian Mysteries' (1961)
Kevin Clinton, 'Myth and Cult: The Iconography of the Eleusinian Mysteries' (1992)
